title: Praline paste
subtitle: Caramelised hazelnuts ground to a smooth, nutty paste
excerpt: Whole hazelnuts coated in hot caramel, set hard, then ground in a food processor until the nut oils are released and the mixture becomes a smooth, rich paste. The base of many patisserie fillings.
sourceNotes: Praline paste (pâte de pralin) is a foundational preparation in French and Belgian patisserie, documented in French confectionery manuals from the eighteenth century. It appears in Urbain Dubois and Emile Bernard's La Cuisine Classique (1856) and in numerous French patisserie texts of the nineteenth century. The technique of coating nuts in caramel, setting hard, then grinding to a paste is unchanged in the modern kitchen. The paste is used as a component in more complex preparations: mousseline, buttercream, ganache, ice cream, and chocolate fillings.

content.text: Caramel reaches approximately 170°C. If it contacts skin, run cold water over the area for at least ten minutes and seek medical attention if needed.
Praline paste is made by cooking a
to a deep amber before stirring in the hazelnuts. Once set hard, the brittle is ground through the stages described in
until a smooth paste forms. The food processor must have a strong motor; a small or cheap processor will overheat and struggle.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Spread the sugar in an even layer in a heavy, wide saucepan over medium heat. Leave undisturbed until the edges begin to melt. Swirl the pan gently to even out the melting. Cook to a deep amber caramel, darker than pale gold, stopping just before it becomes too dark and bitter.

Remove from heat. Add the toasted hazelnuts and salt and stir quickly to coat. Pour immediately onto the lined tray and spread as flat as possible with a spoon. Allow to set completely at room temperature, at least 30 minutes. The brittle will be very hard when fully set.

Break the set brittle into rough pieces and place in a food processor. Pulse to a coarse crumb, then process continuously, scraping down the sides every 2 minutes. Continue until the mixture passes through a fine crumb stage and eventually becomes a smooth, oily paste. This takes 8 to 12 minutes. Transfer to a jar and store refrigerated.
